title: 4x8 Work Table
summary: A full-sheet assembly table: 2x4 frame, plywood shelf, melamine top, six casters. Big enough that a 4x8 sheet lies flat on it — which is the entire point.
description:
Most workbenches are too small for the material woodworking actually comes in. This table is sized to the sheet: a full 4x8 melamine top on a triple-leg-set 2x4 frame, so plywood gets broken down, assemblies get glued up, and finishes get applied on a surface that fits the work. The melamine top is a deliberate choice, not a budget one — glue squeeze-out pops off melamine, and the factory surface is dead flat.

The plan comes in four flavors from one drawing: 30" table height or 36" counter height, rolling or fixed — only the leg lengths change, and all four cut lists ride along in the notes (this file's cut list is the 30" mobile version). Six 4" locking casters make a table this size actually usable in a one-car shop; skip them only if the table will never, ever move.

The build sequence hides a nice trick: the plywood shelf is cut to notch around the legs and then screwed down square — the FACTORY EDGES of the plywood square the frame, instead of you chasing diagonals with a tape. And take the source's warning seriously in step 6: the top 8-foot rails must fit perfectly, because the melamine sheet on top has no flex to forgive a frame that's a half-inch proud.

tools.note: A stack of identical 2x4 crosscuts — batch them against a stop block.
The corner and center notches in the plywood shelf.
Only if your plywood/melamine needs trimming — both go on full-size as shown.

materials.name: Plywood, 5/8" AC, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: The lower shelf — and the frame-squaring jig, as it turns out.

materials.name: Melamine-coated particle board, 3/4", 97" x 49"
materials.note: The top. Melamine sheets run an inch oversize, which gives a handy 1/2" overhang all around.

materials.name: Casters, 4", locking, with screws and washers
materials.note: Omit for the fixed version — the legs just get cut longer.

cutList.note: Caster pads. Omit to gain shelf clearance and set the shelf at the leg bottoms instead.
Cut to fit YOUR frame. Fixed 30": ~18-3/4". Mobile 36": ~20-1/2". Fixed 36": ~24-3/4".
Notched around the legs — cutouts about 3-5/8" x 3-1/2", cut 1/8" generous.

steps.title: Block the leg bottoms
steps.body:
Screw a 3-1/2" 2x4 block to the bottom of each leg with two 2-1/2" screws — these are the caster mounting pads.

Option from the source: skip the blocks entirely and put the bottom shelf at the very bottom of the legs, which buys more clearance under the shelf.

steps.title: Build three leg sets
steps.body: Join the legs in pairs with the 42" cross supports, two screws per joint, making three identical H-shaped leg sets — two ends and a center.

steps.title: Connect with the lower rails
steps.body: Attach the 96" rails to the leg sets, centering the middle leg set. Check the legs are square to the long rails as you go — the 8-foot span amplifies every degree of error into visible lean.

steps.title: Notch and install the shelf — it squares the frame
steps.body:
Cut the corner and center notches out of the plywood shelf with a jigsaw so it drops around the six legs — about 3-5/8" x 3-1/2", cut 1/8" oversize so it slides in without a fight.

Then screw it down with 1-1/2" screws, USING THE PLYWOOD TO SQUARE THE FRAME: the sheet's factory corners are the truest right angles in your shop, so pull the framing to the plywood, not the other way around.

steps.title: Add the upper cross supports
steps.body: Screw the upper 2x4 cross pieces to the tops of the leg sets — the beginnings of the top frame.

steps.title: Fit the upper rails — perfectly
steps.body:
Clamp the upper 96" rails in place before screwing. The source build hit the classic snag here: one leg out of square made the 8-footer 'come up short' — the fix is loosening the leg's screws, pulling it true, and re-driving.

Do not shrug this fit off. The melamine top is a rigid factory rectangle; if the frame under it isn't the same rectangle, the top rocks or overhangs unevenly and there's no trimming it back.

steps.title: Fill the upper legs
steps.body: Cut the six filler pieces to fit your actual openings (the listed 14-1/2" is nominal) and screw them in from the inside so the fasteners don't show on the finished table.

steps.title: Casters, then the top
steps.body:
With help, tip the table and mount the six casters to the leg blocks with their screws and washers. Lock all six before the next part.

Set the melamine sheet on top — at 97" x 49" it overhangs the frame about 1/2" all around. Fasten with a few 1-1/2" screws down through the top; countersink them slightly so nothing stands proud of the work surface. (The source builders left theirs loose, planning to bed it in silicone like a vanity top so it can be replaced when it wears — a reasonable long game for a surface that will take abuse.)
materials: Casters, 4", locking, with screws and washers

images.alt: Large 4x8 rolling work table with 2x4 frame, plywood lower shelf, and white melamine top
